title: Emergency: Dog Eyeball Bulging Out of Socket (Proptosis)
meta_desc: If your dog's eye is bulging, this is a high-urgency medical emergency. Learn about proptosis and why you must see a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Proptosis is a critical medical emergency where the eyeball bulges out of the socket, requiring immediate veterinary intervention to prevent permanent blindness or loss of the eye.

What is proptosis and what causes it in pets?

Proptosis is a serious medical condition where the eyeball is displaced forward, protruding out of the bony eye socket. This usually results in the eyelids being unable to close, leaving the delicate surface of the eye exposed to the air. It is most commonly caused by trauma, such as a fight with another animal, a fall, or an accident.

Is proptosis considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes. This is a high-urgency medical emergency.
  • Time is of the essence; the longer the eye remains out of the socket, the higher the risk of permanent blindness, nerve damage, or loss of the eye itself.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and seek care without delay.

Why should I provide a photo of my pet's eye for triage?

  • Providing a clear photo to your veterinarian or triage professional can be lifesaving in an emergency.
  • A visual assessment allows the medical team to determine the severity of the displacement and check for signs of bleeding or rupture.
  • Clinicians can prepare the necessary surgical equipment or medications before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Traumatic proptosis in dogs typically follows blunt trauma, resulting in globe luxation from the orbit, prevented retraction by eyelid spasms, and further displacement from secondary orbital hemorrhage and swelling. Corneoconjunctival drying and malacia ensue. Prognosis is influenced by pupil size and reflexes, duration of exposure, presence of other globe or orbital damage, breed (brachycephalics are predisposed), and concurrent systemic trauma. Treatment includes providing moisture to lubricate the exposed corneoconjunctiva under general anesthesia, followed by lateral canthotomy and temporary tarsorrhaphy. Systemic antibiotics and corticosteroids are indicated, along with topical antibiotics and mydriatics. The globe should be replaced as soon as possible if the animal's physical condition permits induction of general anesthesia.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1696)

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and try to keep the eye moist using sterile saline solution or a clean, damp cloth. Do not attempt to push the eye back into the socket yourself.

faqs.question: Can the eye be saved?
faqs.answer: Whether the eye can be saved depends on the severity of the injury, the breed of the dog, and how quickly they receive treatment. In many cases, surgery is required to reposition the eye.

faqs.question: Is this more common in certain breeds?
faqs.answer: Yes, brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Pugs, Bulldogs, and Shih Tzus are more prone to proptosis because their eye sockets are naturally shallow.
